text: Hello, I'm sharing an interesting case from a few months ago of a young lady with Crohn's disease who's developed an impassable stricture in the sigmoid colon. Isolated area of disease of the sigmoid colon biopsy is consistent with Crohn's. No other colonic or small bowel disease. Interestingly and completely unrelated, she has full thickness rectal prolapse and I elected to repair that simultaneously. Although for patients with prolapse alone, I no longer do resection rectopexies in favor of rectopexy alone. In this case, I'm doing a resection of the sigmoid for her Crohn's and a rectopexy for the prolapse.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the left ureter before isolating and clipping the superior hemorrhoidal artery. The dissection continues laterally, with attention to the ureter, while addressing the adherence of the rectosigmoid colon to the left pelvic sidewall. The surgeon joins the previously dissected medial plane and works down to the area where the bowel is adherent to the left pelvic sidewall and the left adnexa.

The surgeon performs a full posterior dissection in the TME plane, carefully mobilizing the rectum and sigmoid colon from the left pelvic sidewall. Using a ligature device, the surgeon separates adherent tissue from the left adnexa, ensuring the ureter is not involved. Some pericolic fat and mesorectum are left on the sidewall. The surgeon identifies the distal margin and elevates the structures out of the pelvis, preparing to complete the right lateral and anterior dissection. A deep cul-de-sac is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon places a suture to suspend the uterus, facilitating the dissection. The procedure is performed using a three-port technique with an umbilical Hassan for the ten-millimeter camera, a twelve-millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant, and a five-millimeter trocar in the right mid quadrant. The surgeon re-enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane to continue the anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, using digital palpation to confirm the depth of the dissection. The posterior dissection is then completed.

The surgeon follows the curve of the sacrum to the coccyx, clearing the recto-sigmoid mesentery by placing it under tension. The bowel is exteriorized through a small Pfannenstiel incision, and an anvil is placed for a stapled anastomosis. The proximal mesentery is taken, and the blood supply is checked before dividing distally. A clip is placed at the area of demarcation, visible under white light, before stapling distally.

The surgeon performs a rectopexy using 2-0 silk sutures on an SH needle, taking bites through the periosteum and rectal mesentery. An assistant retracts the bowel over the promontory to facilitate suturing. The surgeon places two sutures, ensuring clear exposure of the periosteum to avoid vascular tissue and potential bleeding. Bipolar energy is used to cauterize visible vessels before suturing.

I didn't use to close this peritoneal opening. I used to leave it and I've actually taken patients back to the operating room, not my own, but Patients have had this operation elsewhere where this wasn't closed and I've had small bowel stuck to raw surfaces down there leading to a bowel obstruction. As well, some of my original postings in this group from several years ago where I didn't close it, I had a lot of commentary from many observers of these cases. that it's a good idea to close it. So ever since then I have been closing it. I've learned from you guys as well. Interestingly, I have a colleague who did one of these cases a few months ago and they told me that the patient developed a bowel obstruction due to the the nubbin of the tail of the V-lock sticking up, which is certainly something that I have seen in a lot of the hernia society posts, and it is one of the concerns of using V-lock. That patient of my colleagues needed to go back to the operating room and ended up with an ileoplectomy due to the way things got trapped due to the adhesions from that little stitch. I used to close these actually just with a vical suture, and obviously it works just fine, and maybe you could eliminate the concern for the adherence to the end of that tail. I try to bury it in the fat and then cover it over with some momentum, and fortunately I haven't experienced any of those issues yet, but obviously it's a concern. I don't know if there's really any right way to do it. This case went very nicely. I was very happy the patient was able to be discharged on post-operative day one. And she's doing very, very well, has not had prolapse since. Again, this is probably more than six months ago now. And all of her obstructive symptoms have resolved and she continues on biologic therapy. Here I'm just doing my leak test, which was negative.
I don't leave drains for any of my anterior resections anymore. I'm very selective in that. And I hope you enjoyed.
